title: Representation, equality and container protocols

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Coordinate implements a safe repr and value equality, returns NotImplemented for unrelated types, and remains immutable before defining hash behavior. Your goal is to implement and test appropriate repr, equality or container protocol behavior without surprising side effects. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scope and closures; Modules and imports; Data classes and value modeling; Composition, inheritance and super. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python special methods let a type participate in representation, equality, hashing, length, containment, and iteration protocols, with each method required to honor the corresponding language contract. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: __repr__ should be unambiguous and safe, __eq__ may return NotImplemented, equal hashable objects need equal hashes, __len__ affects truthiness, and protocol methods should avoid surprising side effects. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Returning False from __eq__ for every unknown type is always correct and defining __eq__ leaves inherited hashing valid automatically.

includes: Implement selected special methods to make domain values predictable in display, comparison and iteration.
Implement and test appropriate repr, equality or container protocol behavior without surprising side effects.
excludes: __repr__ should be unambiguous and safe, __eq__ may return NotImplemented, equal hashable objects need equal hashes, __len__ affects truthiness, and protocol methods should avoid surprising side effects.

outcomes: Implement and test appropriate repr, equality or container protocol behavior without surprising side effects.

examples.title: Representation, equality and container protocols: direct language case
examples.situation: A Coordinate implements a safe repr and value equality, returns NotImplemented for unrelated types, and remains immutable before defining hash behavior.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A Coordinate implements a safe repr and value equality, returns NotImplemented for unrelated types, and remains immutable before defining hash behavior.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Honor each data-model protocol: return NotImplemented for unsupported equality, align equality with hashing, and keep representation and iteration predictable.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Honor each data-model protocol: return NotImplemented for unsupported equality, align equality with hashing, and keep representation and iteration predictable.

examples.title: Representation, equality and container protocols: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A container's __iter__ returns a fresh iterator each call rather than returning one permanently exhausted iterator object.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A container's __iter__ returns a fresh iterator each call rather than returning one permanently exhausted iterator object.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: __repr__ should be unambiguous and safe, __eq__ may return NotImplemented, equal hashable objects need equal hashes, __len__ affects truthiness, and protocol methods should avoid surprising side effects.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Returning False from __eq__ for every unknown type is always correct and defining __eq__ leaves inherited hashing valid automatically.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test appropriate repr, equality or container protocol behavior without surprising side effects.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Returning False from __eq__ for every unknown type is always correct and defining __eq__ leaves inherited hashing valid automatically.
misconceptions.correction: Honor each data-model protocol: return NotImplemented for unsupported equality, align equality with hashing, and keep representation and iteration predictable.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Returning False from __eq__ for every unknown type is always correct and defining __eq__ leaves inherited hashing valid automatically.

analogy.example: a family of service records whose instance state, generated value methods, inheritance order, and protocols define observable behavior can introduce Representation, equality and container protocols, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: classes, data classes, composition, inheritance, and special methods
limitations: descriptor lookup, MRO, hashing, NotImplemented, and shallow frozen state do not map cleanly to physical records
